topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Accessibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Accessibility (Reachability)** in Robot Motion Planning is the formal property describing the **set of all configurations (or states)** that a robot can attain from a given initial configuration through a sequence of admissible motions, subject to **kinematic constraints**, **dynamic constraints**, and **environmental obstacles**.

Mathematically, for a robot operating in a **Configuration Space** $\mathcal{C}$, given an initial configuration $q_0 \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$:

$$
A(q_0) = \{ q \in \mathcal{C}_{free} \;|\; \exists \; \text{continuous, collision-free path } \tau : [0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{free}, \tau(0)=q_0,\; \tau(1)=q \}
$$

The accessibility problem is therefore the question: *"Starting from where I am, what is the set of all places I can legally go to?"*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🧭

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — A Blind Person in a Room with a Flashlight:**
> Imagine you are blindfolded inside an unfamiliar room. You can sweep your flashlight beam (your "sensor footprint") to discover which floor tiles are flat (collision-free) and which contain furniture (obstacles). The **union of all tiles you discover over time** as you keep moving is your *Accessible Set*. Tiles behind the sofa that you can never reach without climbing over it lie outside this set.

In robotic terms:
- **The room** = Workspace $\mathcal{W}$
- **The flashlight beam + your body** = Robot's swept volume in C-space
- **The floor tiles you can stand on** = $\mathcal{C}_{free}$
- **Tiles blocked by furniture** = $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$
- **Tiles reachable by walking** = **Accessible Set** $A(q_0)$

---

## 1.3 Why Accessibility Matters in KTU Robotics

| Domain | Why Accessibility is Critical |
|---|---|
| Industrial Manipulators | Determines whether a target part is **within the reachable workspace** of the arm |
| Autonomous Vehicles | Defines the **safe drivable region** from the current pose |
| Humanoid Robots | Decides whether a footstep on a step is **kinematically feasible** |
| Mobile Manipulators | Combines base + arm reachability to plan **grasp locations** |
| Aerial Swarms | Governs **collision-free region** each drone can claim |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric used in KTU Examinations:** The **Reachability Index** is often defined as the ratio of accessible volume to total free space:
> $$\boxed{\;\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{\mu(A(q_0))}{\mu(\mathcal{C}_{free})}\;}$$
> where $\mu(\cdot)$ is the **Lebesgue measure** (volume/area) in the appropriate dimension.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

- **Configuration $q$** — A complete specification of the robot's pose (e.g., joint angles for an arm, $x,y,\theta$ for a mobile robot).
- **Configuration Space $\mathcal{C}$** — The **manifold** of all possible configurations.
- **Free Space $\mathcal{C}_{free}$** — $\mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ (collision-free subset).
- **Obstacle Region $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$** — Pre-image of workspace obstacles under the robot's geometry mapping.
- **Connected Component of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$** — A maximal region of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ where any two points are linked by a collision-free path. Accessibility from $q_0$ is restricted to the **component containing $q_0$**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Tiers of Accessibility

Accessibility is not a single concept — it exists in **three escalating layers of complexity** that every KTU student must master:

### Tier 1: Geometric / Kinematic Accessibility
- Considers **only position and orientation**, no velocities or forces.
- The robot is a **rigid body** in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.
- **Reachable Workspace** is the projection of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ back to workspace.

### Tier 2: Differential / Kinodynamic Accessibility
- Considers **velocity and acceleration limits** of each joint or wheel.
- Governed by the **control-affine dynamics** $\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)u$.
- The accessible set **shrinks** because not all of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is dynamically reachable in a given time horizon $T$.

### Tier 3: Topological / Connectivity Accessibility
- Considers whether the **connected component** of $q_0$ contains the goal $q_{goal}$.
- If $\text{Comp}(q_0) \neq \text{Comp}(q_{goal})$, **no path exists** — the problem is **infeasible**.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Accessibility Computation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-step logic** for computing the accessible set $A(q_0)$ in a planning problem:

1. **Step 1 — Build Configuration Space:** Parameterize the robot by $q \in \mathcal{C}$. For an $n$-DOF arm, $q = (q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (or $\mathbb{T}^n$ for revolute joints).
2. **Step 2 — Map Workspace Obstacles to C-space:** For each workspace obstacle $\mathcal{O}_i \subseteq \mathcal{W}$, compute the **C-obstacle**:
$$\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \bigcup_{i} \{ q \in \mathcal{C} \;|\; \text{Robot}(q) \cap \mathcal{O}_i \neq \emptyset \}$$
3. **Step 3 — Define Free Space:** $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$.
4. **Step 4 — Wavefront Expansion:** Starting from $q_0$, propagate outward through $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ using a graph search (BFS, Dijkstra, A*, RRT) until all reachable vertices are visited or the goal is found.
5. **Step 5 — Extract Accessible Set:** $A(q_0)$ is the visited set. Connectivity test: if $q_{goal} \notin A(q_0)$, declare **infeasibility**.

---

## 2.3 Accessibility for Non-Holonomic Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-Holonomic Constraint:** A velocity constraint $\omega(q)\dot{q} = 0$ that is **not integrable**, meaning the robot cannot move instantaneously in arbitrary directions (e.g., a car can move forward/backward but not sideways).

For a unicycle-like mobile robot with state $q = (x, y, \theta)$ and controls $(v, \omega)$:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

The accessibility is constrained to a **2-D manifold in a 3-D state space**. A critical result:

> **Manhattan-like motions (drive → rotate → drive)** are needed to access arbitrary $(x,y)$ positions because pure translation is impossible sideways.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All symbols must be memorized.** The vertical bar in any expression is typeset as `\vert` to preserve markdown table syntax.

| # | Concept | Equation | Notes / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Configuration Space | $\mathcal{C} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \mathcal{C}_i$ | $n$ = number of DOF |
| 2 | Free Space | $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ | Where robot can exist |
| 3 | C-Obstacle (rigid body) | $\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \{ q \in \mathcal{C} \vert \mathcal{R}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset \}$ | $\mathcal{R}(q)$ = robot at config $q$ |
| 4 | Accessible Set | $A(q_0) = \{ q \in \mathcal{C}_{free} \vert \exists \text{ path } \tau \text{ from } q_0 \text{ to } q \}$ | Path must be continuous |
| 5 | Reachability Index | $\mathcal{R} = \mu(A(q_0)) \big/ \mu(\mathcal{C}_{free})$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le \mathcal{R} \le 1$ |
| 6 | Reachable Workspace | $\mathcal{W}_{reach} = \pi(\mathcal{C}_{free})$ | $\pi$ = forward kinematics projection |
| 7 | Dexterous Workspace | $\mathcal{W}_{dex} = \{ p \in \mathcal{W}_{reach} \vert \text{end-effector has full orientation}\}$ | Subset of $\mathcal{W}_{reach}$ |
| 8 | Time-Bounded Accessibility | $A(q_0, T) = \{ x(T) \vert \dot{x} = f(x,u),\; x(0)=q_0,\; u \in \mathcal{U} \}$ | Kinodynamic version |
| 9 | Non-Holonomic Drift Field | $\dot{q} = g_1(q)u_1 + g_2(q)u_2$ | Car: $g_1 = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta, 0)^T$ |
| 10 | LFS (Lie Algebra Rank) Condition | $\text{rank}[\,g_1,\,g_2,\, [g_1,g_2]\,] = n$ | Sufficient for local accessibility |
| 11 | Path Length Cost | $L(\tau) = \int_0^1 \lVert \dot{\tau}(s) \rVert \, ds$ | For optimality in $A(q_0)$ |
| 12 | Holonomic Condition | $\omega(q)\dot{q} = 0$ non-integrable ⟹ non-holonomic | Car sideways: blocked |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Accessibility analysis is the **first sanity check** in every industrial robotics deployment. Without it:
> - A welding robot may be **installed out of reach** of every part on the assembly line.
> - An autonomous forklift may **plan a path that is geometrically free but kinematically impossible** (e.g., trying to turn in zero radius).
> - A surgical robot's end-effector may **never reach a deep-cavity target** if port placement is poorly chosen.

In **NASA's Mars rovers**, accessibility analysis determines which rocks and craters the rover can physically approach given its rocker-bogie suspension geometry. In **warehouse automation** (Amazon Kiva robots), the accessible set of every pod-carrying robot is recomputed **40 times per second** to coordinate traffic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Accessible Set of a 2-DOF Planar Arm ⭐ (High-Yield KTU Problem)

> **Problem Setup:** A 2-DOF planar manipulator has link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$ (both constant), joint limits $0 \le q_1 \le 2\pi$ and $-\pi/2 \le q_2 \le \pi/2$, and a circular workspace obstacle of radius $r_{obs}$ centered at $(x_c, y_c)$. Compute the accessible set $A(q_0)$.

### Step 1 — Forward Kinematics

The end-effector position in workspace is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(q_1, q_2) &= L_1 \cos(q_1) + L_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) \\
y(q_1, q_2) &= L_1 \sin(q_1) + L_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **forward kinematics map** $\pi : \mathcal{C} \to \mathcal{W}$. Each configuration $q = (q_1, q_2)$ maps to exactly one workspace point $(x, y)$.

### Step 2 — Compute the Reachable Workspace (No Obstacles)

For a point on the boundary of the reachable workspace, we set up:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^2 + y^2 &= (L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2))^2 + (L_1 \sin q_1 + L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2))^2 \\
&= L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos(q_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Using the identity $\cos(q_1)\cos(q_1+q_2) + \sin(q_1)\sin(q_1+q_2) = \cos(q_2)$.

So the **maximum reach** is $L_1 + L_2$ (when $q_2 = 0$), and the **minimum reach** is $|L_1 - L_2|$ (when $q_2 = \pi$).

### Step 3 — Annular Reachable Workspace (No Obstacle)

$$
\mathcal{W}_{reach} = \{ (x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \;\vert\; \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert^2 \le x^2 + y^2 \le (L_1 + L_2)^2 \}
$$

This is an **annulus** of inner radius $|L_1 - L_2|$ and outer radius $L_1 + L_2$.

### Step 4 — C-Obstacle Construction

For a circular obstacle of radius $r_{obs}$ centered at $(x_c, y_c)$, the C-obstacle is the set of configurations where the end-effector intersects it:

$$
\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \left\{ (q_1, q_2) \in \mathcal{C} \;\Bigg|\; \left(x(q_1, q_2) - x_c\right)^2 + \left(y(q_1, q_2) - y_c\right)^2 \le r_{obs}^2 \right\}
$$

### Step 5 — Free Space and Accessible Set

$$
\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}
$$

$$
A(q_0) = \text{ConnectedComponent}(q_0, \mathcal{C}_{free})
$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Illustration

Let $L_1 = 2$, $L_2 = 1.5$, $r_{obs} = 0.5$, center $(x_c, y_c) = (1.5, 0)$.

- **Outer reach:** $L_1 + L_2 = 3.5$ units
- **Inner reach:** $L_1 - L_2 = 0.5$ units
- The obstacle is at distance $1.5$ from origin → **inside the annulus**
- The C-obstacle is a curved "banana-shaped" region in the $(q_1, q_2)$ torus.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Accessibility of a Differential-Drive Robot

> **Problem:** A differential-drive robot has state $q = (x, y, \theta)$ and controls $(v, \omega)$ with $|v| \le v_{max}$, $|\omega| \le \omega_{max}$. Find the **reachable set after time $T$**.

### Step 1 — System Dynamics

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

### Step 2 — Controllability Matrix (Chow–Rashevskii Theorem)

The control vector fields are:

$$
g_1 = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad g_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Their **Lie bracket** $[g_1, g_2] = \frac{\partial g_2}{\partial q} g_1 - \frac{\partial g_1}{\partial q} g_2$:

$$
[g_1, g_2] = -\frac{\partial g_1}{\partial q} g_2 = \begin{pmatrix} -\sin\theta \\ \cos\theta \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Rank Condition

$$
\text{rank}\big[ g_1,\; g_2,\; [g_1, g_2] \big] = \text{rank}\begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} = 3
$$

Since the rank equals $n = 3$ (the dimension of state), the system satisfies the **LARC (Lie Algebra Rank Condition)** ⟹ the system is **locally controllable** everywhere except at singular points.

### Step 4 — Reachable Set Approximation (Reeds–Shepp Curves)

In the unbounded case ($v_{max}, \omega_{max}$ unlimited), the shortest paths are **Reeds–Shepp curves** consisting of at most 5 segments (CSC, CCC, etc.). The reachable set after traveling a total path length $S = v_{max} T$ is:

$$
A(q_0, T) \subseteq \{ (x,y,\theta) \;\vert\; x^2 + y^2 \le (v_{max} T)^2 \text{ (upper bound disc)} \}
$$

with the orientation $\theta$ constrained by **how much the robot can turn** during the maneuver.

### Step 5 — Turning Radius Constraint

Minimum turning radius: $R_{min} = v_{max} / \omega_{max}$

Any configuration whose displacement from $q_0$ is **less than** some multiple of $R_{min}$ in the wrong direction may be **inaccessible** at time $T$ — this is a **key non-holonomic limitation**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reachability Index for a Mobile Robot in a Rectangular Hallway

> **Problem:** A point robot moves in a 5 m × 4 m rectangular room starting at corner $(0,0)$. Find the reachability index $\mathcal{R}$.

**Solution Logic:**

- Total free space area: $\mu(\mathcal{C}_{free}) = 5 \times 4 = 20 \, m^2$ (no obstacles).
- A point robot has **no orientation constraint** ⟹ it is **fully holonomic**.
- Therefore the entire room is accessible: $A(q_0) = \mathcal{C}_{free}$, $\mu(A(q_0)) = 20$.
- Reachability Index: $\mathcal{R} = 20/20 = 1.0$ ⟹ **Fully accessible**.

> This is the **maximum possible value**, illustrating that a holonomic point robot with no obstacles has $\mathcal{R} = 1$ always.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Accessibility Decision Table

| Robot Type | Dimension of $\mathcal{C}$ | Obstacle Effect | Non-Holonomic? | Typical $\mathcal{R}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2-DOF Planar Arm | 2 | Curved C-obstacles | No | 0.6 – 0.9 |
| 6-DOF Industrial Arm | 6 | Complex polytopes | No | 0.4 – 0.7 |
| Differential Drive | 3 | Workspace polygons | **Yes** | 0.3 – 0.6 |
| Car-like (Ackermann) | 3 (or 4 with vel) | Lane boundaries | **Yes** | 0.2 – 0.5 |
| Quadrotor (with attitude) | 12 | 3-D obstacles | No (fully actuated) | 0.7 – 0.9 |
| Holonomic Point Robot | 2 (or 3) | Workspace obstacles | No | ≈ 1.0 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Accessibility Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workspace W and Robot Geometry R] --> B[Forward Kinematics Map pi]
    B --> C[Configuration Space C]
    D[Workspace Obstacles O] --> E[Inverse Pre-image Mapping]
    C --> E
    E --> F[C-Obstacles C_obs]
    C --> G[C minus C_obs]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Free Space C_free]
    H --> I[Graph Search BFS or A*]
    I0[Initial Config q0] --> I
    I --> J[Accessible Set A of q0]
    I --> K[Goal Config q_goal]
    J --> L{Is q_goal inside A of q0?}
    K --> L
    L -->|Yes| M[Path Exists - Proceed to Trajectory Optimization]
    L -->|No| N[Infeasible - Re-plan with New Start or Modified Environment]
```

---

## 4.2 Accessibility Tier Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Tier 1 - Geometric Accessibility] --> L2[Tier 2 - Differential Accessibility]
    L2 --> L3[Tier 3 - Topological Connectivity]
    L1a[Position and Orientation Only] --> L1
    L2a[Velocity and Acceleration Bounds] --> L2
    L3a[Connected Components of C_free] --> L3
    L1 --> R1[Reachable Workspace W_reach]
    L2 --> R2[Time-bounded Reachable Set A q0 T]
    L3 --> R3[Feasibility Verdict]
```

---

## 4.3 C-space Decomposition for Accessibility Analysis

```mermaid
graph LR
    Q[Full Configuration Space C] --> OBS[C-Obstacles Region]
    Q --> FREE[Free Space C_free]
    FREE --> CC1[Connected Component 1 - Contains q0]
    FREE --> CC2[Connected Component 2 - Contains q_goal]
    FREE --> CC3[Disconnected Component 3 - Isolated Pocket]
    CC1 --> ACC[Accessible Set A of q0]
    CC2 --> NG[Not Accessible from q0]
    CC3 --> NG
```

---

## 4.4 Non-Holonomic Accessibility Constraints (Car-like Robot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial Pose q0] --> B[Drive Forward with phi = 0]
    B --> C[Stop and Rotate phi]
    C --> D[Drive Forward to Target x y]
    D --> E[Final Pose with Correct theta]
    B -.->|Min Turning Radius R_min = L/tan phi_max| R[Constraint: No Lateral Motion Allowed]
    R --> B
    R --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Diagram Rationale:** Physical schematic drawings of C-space manifolds and reachable workspaces would require complex 2-D/3-D illustrations. The above flowcharts map the **functional information flow** of accessibility computation, which is the more examinable representation in KTU 2024 Scheme theory papers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🎯 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Accessibility" in the context of robot motion planning. How is it different from reachability of the end-effector?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Accessibility** is the property of a configuration (or set of configurations) to be attainable from an initial configuration via a **continuous, collision-free, kinematically feasible** trajectory within the configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.

**Difference from end-effector reachability:**

| Aspect | Accessibility | End-Effector Reachability |
|---|---|---|
| Defined in | Configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ | Task / Workspace $\mathcal{W}$ |
| Considers | Whole robot body + obstacles | Only TCP position/orientation |
| Accounts for | Collision, joint limits, dynamics | Geometry, joint limits only |
| Use | Feasibility of motion plans | Pre-deployment cell design |

Accessibility is the **broader, planning-level concept**, while end-effector reachability is a **geometric, pre-deployment metric**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the Lie Algebra Rank Condition (LARC) and explain its role in determining accessibility of non-holonomic systems.**

**Model Answer:**

> **LARC:** A control-affine system $\dot{x} = f(x) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} g_i(x) u_i$ is **locally accessible** from $x_0$ if the **smallest Lie algebra** generated by the vector fields $\{g_1, \dots, g_m, f\}$ has **full rank $n$** (equal to the dimension of the state space) at $x_0$.

**Role in accessibility:**

- LARC gives a **necessary (and, for analytic systems, sufficient) condition** for local accessibility.
- For driftless systems (e.g., a car), it requires evaluating **iterated Lie brackets** of the input vector fields, which physically represent **higher-order maneuvers** (e.g., parallel parking via forward-reverse sequences).
- If LARC fails at some $x_0$, the system is **not locally accessible** there, meaning the robot is "stuck" in a lower-dimensional manifold.

**Example:** A differential-drive robot satisfies LARC because $[g_1, g_2]$ is linearly independent of $g_1$ and $g_2$, so it can reach any nearby $(x, y, \theta)$ — confirming the empirical fact that parallel parking is possible.

---

## 🎯 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With suitable diagrams, explain the construction of configuration space obstacles for a **circular mobile robot of radius $r$** translating in a 2-D workspace containing a single rectangular obstacle. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Define the **accessible set** $A(q_0)$ of a robot in its configuration space. A 2-DOF planar arm has link lengths $L_1 = 1\,\text{m}$, $L_2 = 0.8\,\text{m}$, and joint limits $0 \le q_1 \le 2\pi$, $-\pi/2 \le q_2 \le \pi/2$. Compute the **maximum reach**, **minimum reach**, and the **reachable workspace area** when no obstacles are present. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Setup:**
- Robot: disc of radius $r$, position $(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$.
- Workspace obstacle: rectangle with corners $(x_{\min}, y_{\min})$ and $(x_{\max}, y_{\max})$.
- Configuration space: $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$.

**Step 2 — C-obstacle Construction via Minkowski Sum:**

The C-obstacle is the set of robot center positions where the robot intersects the rectangle. This is obtained by **inflating the obstacle** by the robot's radius $r$ (a Minkowski sum with a disc):

$$
\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \mathcal{O} \oplus B(0, r)
$$

where $B(0, r)$ is a disc of radius $r$ centered at the origin.

**Step 3 — Resulting Shape:**
The rectangular obstacle becomes a **rounded rectangle** — a rectangle of the same dimensions with **four quarter-circle caps of radius $r$** at the corners:

$$
\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \left\{ (x,y) \;\Bigg|\; \begin{array}{l} x_{\min} - r \le x \le x_{\max} + r, \\ y_{\min} - r \le y \le y_{\max} + r, \\ \text{or } (x - x_c)^2 + (y - y_c)^2 \le r^2 \text{ for each corner} \end{array} \right\}
$$

**Step 4 — Free Space:**
$\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$.

**Diagram (Schematic):**

```
   _____________________
  /                     \
 |   <-- C_obs (rounded   |
 |       rectangle)       |
  \_____________________/
        
   Free space C_free surrounds the inflated obstacle
```

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Recap Formulas:**

Maximum reach: $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2$

Minimum reach: $R_{min} = |L_1 - L_2|$ (with $L_1 > L_2$, this is $L_1 - L_2$)

**Step 2 — Compute Numerical Values:**

$$
R_{max} = 1.0 + 0.8 = 1.8 \, \text{m}
$$

$$
R_{min} = 1.0 - 0.8 = 0.2 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Reachable Workspace Area:**

The reachable workspace is an **annulus** of inner radius $0.2$ m and outer radius $1.8$ m. Its area is:

$$
A_{ws} = \pi (R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2) = \pi (1.8^2 - 0.2^2) = \pi (3.24 - 0.04) = 3.2 \pi \approx 10.05 \, \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Reachability Index (No Obstacle):**

Since the entire annulus is reachable (no obstacles), $\mathcal{R} = 1.0$ for the relative workspace, but the absolute reachability index **with respect to all of $\mathbb{R}^2$** is $0$ (because of the unreachable hole in the middle and points beyond $1.8$ m).

**Final Answers:**
- $R_{max} = 1.8$ m
- $R_{min} = 0.2$ m
- $A_{ws} = 3.2\pi \approx 10.05$ m²

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** What is meant by the **reachable workspace** of a robot? Differentiate between **reachable workspace**, **dexterous workspace**, and **accessible workspace**, with one example for each. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** For a **unicycle mobile robot** with dynamics $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$, evaluate the **Lie Algebra Rank Condition** at an arbitrary state $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0)$. Comment on the implications for accessibility. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Reachable Workspace (RW):**
The set of all positions (and possibly orientations) that the **end-effector** can attain for **at least one** robot configuration.

Example: The **annulus** of a 2-DOF arm with link lengths $L_1, L_2$.

**Step 2 — Dexterous Workspace (DW):**
The subset of RW where the end-effector can attain **arbitrary orientations** (i.e., full $SO(2)$ or $SO(3)$ rotation).

Example: For a 3-DOF planar arm with $L_1 \neq L_2 \neq L_3$, only the **central region** inside the largest annulus is dexterous.

**Step 3 — Accessible Workspace (AW):**
A **planning-level** concept — the set of configurations (not just end-effector poses) that the robot can be in **without collision**, starting from a given initial state. It accounts for the **entire body** of the robot, not just the TCP.

Example: A 6-DOF arm in a cluttered bin-picking cell — its AW may be only a fraction of its RW because the elbow can collide with bins or conveyors.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Reachable WS | Dexterous WS | Accessible WS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Considers | TCP geometry | TCP orientation | Whole robot body |
| Includes obstacles | No | No | **Yes** |
| Size relative to each other | Largest | Smallest subset of RW | Subset of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ |

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Define the Control Vector Fields:**
With state $q = (x, y, \theta)^T$ and controls $u_1 = v$, $u_2 = \omega$:

$$
g_1 = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad g_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Lie Bracket $[g_1, g_2]$:**

$$
[g_1, g_2] = \frac{\partial g_2}{\partial q} g_1 - \frac{\partial g_1}{\partial q} g_2
$$

Since $g_2$ is constant, $\partial g_2 / \partial q = 0$. Therefore:

$$
[g_1, g_2] = -\frac{\partial g_1}{\partial q} g_2
$$

The Jacobian of $g_1$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial g_1}{\partial q} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & -\sin\theta \\ 0 & 0 & \cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Multiplying by $g_2 = (0, 0, 1)^T$:

$$
[g_1, g_2] = -\begin{pmatrix} -\sin\theta \\ \cos\theta \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \sin\theta \\ -\cos\theta \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the Distribution Matrix:**

$$
\mathcal{D} = \big[\, g_1,\; g_2,\; [g_1, g_2] \,\big] = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & 0 & -\cos\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Determinant:**

$$
\det(\mathcal{D}) = 0 \cdot (\cdots) - 0 \cdot (\cdots) + 1 \cdot (\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) = 1
$$

The determinant is **identically equal to $1$** at every state $(x, y, \theta)$ — **non-zero everywhere**.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

Since $\det(\mathcal{D}) = 1 \neq 0$, the rank is $3$ everywhere, satisfying LARC. Therefore, the unicycle is **locally accessible at every point** — the robot can reach any nearby state by combining translations and rotations cleverly (e.g., "wiggle" motions like parallel parking). However, **global accessibility** is still constrained by control bounds $|v| \le v_{max}$, $|\omega| \le \omega_{max}$ and the **minimum turning radius** $R_{min} = v_{max} / \omega_{max}$.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Confusing Reachable Workspace with Accessible Workspace** — RW is a geometric pre-deployment metric, AW is a planning-time collision-aware set. Examiners allocate **2 marks** for distinguishing them.
> 2. **Forgetting that non-holonomic systems satisfy LARC but are NOT omnidirectionally accessible in finite time** — LARC only guarantees *local* accessibility, not *global instantaneous* reachability.
> 3. **Sign errors in Lie bracket computation** — always re-check whether you are computing $[g_1, g_2]$ or $[g_2, g_1]$ (they differ by a sign).
> 4. **Ignoring Minkowski sum direction** — for a translating robot, the obstacle is **inflated by the robot's shape**, not the other way around. Many students draw the un-inflated obstacle, losing 3 marks in Part (a) of such problems.
> 5. **Forgetting joint limits** — when computing $R_{min}$ of a 2-DOF arm, students often write $|L_1 - L_2|$ without checking whether $q_2 = \pi$ is actually within joint limits. The effective $R_{min}$ may be larger.
> 6. **Mixing workspace and configuration space** — never quote a workspace area when the question asks for a C-space volume. Units and dimensions are checked by the examiner.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Accessibility** = set of all configurations reachable via continuous, collision-free, kinematically feasible paths from $q_0$.
- ✅ **Configuration Space $\mathcal{C}$** parameterizes the entire robot; **Free Space $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$** is where valid motion lives.
- ✅ **C-obstacles** are obtained by **inflating workspace obstacles** by the robot's geometry (Minkowski sum for translation, more complex for rotation).
- ✅ **Three tiers**: Geometric → Differential (kinodynamic) → Topological (connectivity).
- ✅ **LARC** (Chow–Rashevskii Theorem) determines local accessibility of non-holonomic systems by checking the rank of the distribution spanned by the input vector fields and their iterated Lie brackets.
- ✅ **Holonomic robots** (e.g., aerial manipulators) have $\mathcal{R} \approx 1$ in open space; **non-holonomic robots** (cars, unicycles) have $\mathcal{R} < 1$ even in open space.
- ✅ **Reachable Workspace** ⊇ **Dexterous Workspace**; **Accessible Set** ⊆ **$\mathcal{C}_{free}$**.
- ✅ **Reachability Index** $\mathcal{R} = \mu(A(q_0)) / \mu(\mathcal{C}_{free})$ — a key KTU high-yield formula.
- ✅ A goal is **reachable** ⟺ it lies in the **same connected component** of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ as $q_0$.
- ✅ **Reeds–Shepp curves** describe shortest paths for car-like robots; **Dubins curves** for the no-reverse special case.
- ✅ **Minimum turning radius** $R_{min} = L / \tan(\phi_{max})$ for Ackermann-steered vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Departability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Decidability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robot Motion Planning**, *Decidability* refers to the formal property that asks a fundamental question:

> *Does there exist a finite, terminating algorithm that, given a complete mathematical description of the robot, the obstacles, the start configuration, and the goal configuration, can definitively answer YES or NO (and output a path if YES) regarding the existence of a collision-free motion?*

Formally, the **General Motion Planning Problem (GMPP)** — also called the **Piano Mover's Problem** — is the decision problem:

> Given a robot $\mathcal{R}$, a workspace $\mathcal{W} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ with obstacles $\mathcal{O}_1, \mathcal{O}_2, \dots, \mathcal{O}_n$, an initial configuration $\mathbf{q}_{init}$, and a goal configuration $\mathbf{q}_{goal}$, decide whether there exists a continuous collision-free path $\tau: [0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{free}$ such that $\tau(0) = \mathbf{q}_{init}$ and $\tau(1) = \mathbf{q}_{goal}$.

The problem is **decidable** if such an algorithm exists. However, decidability does **not** imply tractability — it merely guarantees that an answer exists in finite time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Decidability is treated separately from computational complexity. A problem may be *decidable* yet *intractable* (e.g., PSPACE-hard). This distinction is a favourite 14-mark question in KTU ESE.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are moving a large L-shaped sofa (the famous *piano mover's problem*) through a narrow, cluttered hallway with irregularly shaped furniture blocking the way.

- **Decidability Question:** *Is it mathematically GUARANTEED that, given enough reasoning time, you can determine whether the sofa can be moved from Room A to Room B without lifting it?*
- The answer is **YES**, because the hallway and obstacles are described by finite algebraic equations, and the configuration space is a finite-dimensional manifold.

Now, the follow-up question is: *How long will this reasoning take?* If the answer is "exponential in the number of obstacles," the problem is still decidable but practically infeasible for large inputs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Decidability ≠ Tractability**
> - **Decidable** = A correct answer exists in finite time (theoretical guarantee).
> - **Tractable** = The answer can be found in polynomial time (practical feasibility).

## 1.3 Historical Anchor

The cornerstone result was established by **John H. Reif (1979)** in his seminal paper *"Complexity of the Mover's Problem and Generalizations."* Reif proved that:

> The General Motion Planning Problem is **PSPACE-hard**, and when restricted to polyhedral obstacles, it is **PSPACE-complete**.

This is the foundational result upon which all KTU-level decidability analysis rests.

## 1.4 Key Boundary Conditions

For the motion planning problem to be well-posed and decidable, the following must hold:

| Symbol | Boundary Condition | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| $n$ | Finite, $n \in \mathbb{N}$ | Number of obstacle vertices/faces must be finite |
| $d$ | Fixed dimension | $\mathcal{C}$-space is $\mathbb{R}^d$ or a manifold thereof |
| $\mathcal{R}$ | Semi-algebraic set | Robot and obstacles must be expressible by polynomial inequalities |
| $L$ | Bit-length finite | Input size $L$ bounds the algorithmic search space |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the obstacles are described by **non-computable functions** (e.g., involving the halting oracle), the problem becomes **undecidable** (e.g., the *Generalized Mover's Problem* with non-algebraic constraints, studied by Reif as well).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Decision Problem Formulation

To rigorously discuss decidability, the motion planning problem must be phrased as a **formal language recognition problem**:

$$
\text{MOVE} = \{ \langle \mathcal{R}, \mathcal{O}, \mathbf{q}_{init}, \mathbf{q}_{goal}, 1^k \rangle \mid \exists \text{ a collision-free path of complexity } \leq k\}
$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{R}$ encodes the robot geometry.
- $\mathcal{O}$ encodes the obstacle geometry.
- $1^k$ is a tally string bounding the path complexity (length, smoothness, or bit-precision).
- $\langle \cdot \rangle$ denotes the binary encoding of the input.

Membership in the language MOVE means: *"YES, a path exists of complexity at most $k$."*

## 2.2 Hierarchy of Sub-Problems (Reif's Framework)

The complexity depends drastically on the robot and obstacle class. KTU expects students to know the following layered hierarchy:

- **Step 1 — Algebraic Decision:** Inputs are polynomials with rational coefficients of degree at most $d$ and integer bit-length $L$.
- **Step 2 — Configuration Space Construction:** $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ is a semi-algebraic set defined by polynomial inequalities.
- **Step 3 — Path Existence Test:** Connectedness of the connected component of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ containing $\mathbf{q}_{init}$.
- **Step 4 — Complexity Bound:** Time/space required as a function of $n$ and $L$.

## 2.3 Core Theorems (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Theorem | Statement | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | **Reif's Decidability Theorem** | GMPP is decidable for any finite collection of semi-algebraic obstacles in $\mathbb{R}^d$. | Reif (1979) |
| T2 | **Reif's Hardness Theorem** | GMPP is PSPACE-hard, even for polyhedral obstacles in 3D. | Reif (1979) |
| T3 | **Schwartz-Sharir Cell Decomposition** | A cylindrical algebraic decomposition of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ exists and is computable. | Schwartz & Sharir (1983) |
| T4 | **Canny's Roadmap Theorem** | A roadmap of size $(sd)^{O(n)}$ exists in $\mathbb{R}^d$ with $s$ polynomials of degree $n$. | Canny (1988) |
| T5 | **NP-hardness of 2D Point Robot** | Moving a point through $n$ polygons in 2D is NP-hard (without coord. bounds). | — |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| Formula / Symbol | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| C-space dimension | $d = \sum_{i=1}^{r} d_i$ | Sum of joint DOFs for $r$ rigid links |
| Input size | $L$ | Total bit-length of the encoded problem |
| Number of obstacles | $n$ | Number of algebraic constraints |
| Canny Roadmap bound | $(sd)^{O(n)}$ | Size of the roadmap in $\mathbb{R}^d$ |
| Schwartz-Sharir bound | $L^{O(1)} \cdot (sd)^{O(n)}$ | Time complexity of cell decomposition |
| Reif complexity | $\mathrm{PSPACE}$ | Space class of GMPP |
| 2D Point Robot | $\mathrm{NP\text{-}hard}$ | Lower bound for polygon obstacles |
| Configuration free space | $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \{ \mathbf{q} \mid \mathcal{R}(\mathbf{q}) \cap \mathcal{O} = \emptyset\}$ | Definition |
| Configuration obstacle | $\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \{ \mathbf{q} \mid \mathcal{R}(\mathbf{q}) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset\}$ | Definition |
| Path connectivity test | $[\mathbf{q}_{init}] \stackrel{?}{=} [\mathbf{q}_{goal}]$ | In $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ quotient |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Robotic Arms:** Knowing that exact algorithms are doubly-exponential in worst case motivates sampling-based planners (RRT, PRM) used in ROS.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Decidability results justify why provably-correct planning is impossible in real-time for urban-scale problems, justifying hierarchical planners.
- **CAD Verification:** Roadmap algorithms (Canny's) enable assembly-disassembly sequence verification in mechanical design.
- **Game AI & Animation:** Path-planning complexity bounds inform level design heuristics.

## 2.6 The "Why" Behind PSPACE-Hardness

The PSPACE-hardness arises because GMPP can simulate the computation of a **Turing Machine**:

- Each joint configuration corresponds to a TM tape head state.
- Obstacles encode the TM transition rules.
- A collision-free path corresponds to a valid TM computation accepting the input.

Since simulating a polynomial-space TM is required, GMPP inherits PSPACE-hardness.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU: If asked *"Why is GMPP PSPACE-hard?"*, the model answer is: *"Because the configuration space is exponential in the input size, and any path may require exponential computation steps to verify, while the entire search fits in polynomial space."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Lower Bound on Roadmap Size

We derive the roadmap size lower bound for the General Motion Planning Problem.

**Setup:**
- Let $\mathcal{W} = \mathbb{R}^d$.
- Let the free space $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ be defined by $s$ polynomial inequalities of maximum degree $n$.
- Let the robot have $r$ degrees of freedom.

**Goal:** Find a lower bound on the number of cells required in any cell decomposition of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.

**Step 1 — Parameterize the problem.**
The input is described by $s$ polynomials $p_i(\mathbf{x})$ with $1 \leq i \leq s$, each of degree at most $n$. The total number of real roots of these polynomials along any line is bounded by $sn$.

**Step 2 — Apply Warren's Theorem (Algebraic Lower Bound).**
A foundational result in real algebraic geometry states that the number of connected components of a semi-algebraic set defined by $s$ polynomials of degree $n$ in $\mathbb{R}^d$ is bounded by:

$$
\mathcal{B}(s, d, n) = (sd)^{O(n)}
$$

This is a doubly-exponential growth in $d$, and exponential in $n$.

**Step 3 — Apply to Configuration Space.**
For the motion planning problem, $d$ is replaced by the configuration space dimension $r$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of cells in } \mathcal{C}_{free} \text{ decomposition} &\leq (sr)^{O(n)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the algorithmic complexity.**
Any exact algorithm must, in the worst case, visit each cell. So time complexity is:

$$
T(L) = L^{O(1)} \cdot (sr)^{O(n)}
$$

Where the $L^{O(1)}$ factor accounts for polynomial arithmetic on inputs of bit-length $L$.

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(L) &= L^{O(1)} \cdot (sr)^{O(n)} \\
     &= 2^{O(n \log(sr) + \log L)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **exponential in the polynomial degree $n$** but **polynomial in the bit-length $L$**. This matches the empirical observation: planning is hard when *geometric complexity* is high, but tractable when the input is large but algebraically simple.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For a 6-DOF rigid body in a workspace with 10 obstacles defined by degree-2 polynomials (cubes, spheres, cylinders), the roadmap may have $(10 \cdot 6)^{O(2)} = 60^{O(2)} \approx 3600$ cells — very tractable. For higher-degree algebraic surfaces (degree 10), this explodes to $60^{10}$, which is impractical — motivating sampling-based methods.

## 3.2 Derivation: Reduction from TQBF to GMPP

This shows the PSPACE-hardness constructively.

**Step 1 — Choose a PSPACE-complete source problem.**
The **True Quantified Boolean Formula (TQBF)** problem:

$$
\Phi = \forall x_1 \exists x_2 \forall x_3 \dots Q_k x_k \; \varphi(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_k)
$$

is PSPACE-complete (Stockmeyer & Meyer, 1973).

**Step 2 — Encode $\Phi$ as an obstacle field.**
Each variable $x_i$ corresponds to a "channel" in the configuration space. The robot's $i$-th joint position selects the truth value of $x_i$. Obstacles encode the Boolean formula $\varphi$.

**Step 3 — Encode quantifiers as corridor topology.**
- A $\forall x_i$ quantifier creates a **closed loop** corridor (the robot must be able to traverse both $x_i = 0$ and $x_i = 1$).
- An $\exists x_i$ quantifier creates a **branching fork** (only one branch need be taken).

**Step 4 — Show equivalence.**
A collision-free path from $\mathbf{q}_{init}$ to $\mathbf{q}_{goal}$ exists **if and only if** $\Phi$ is TRUE.

$$
\Phi \text{ is TRUE} \iff \exists \text{ collision-free path } \tau \in \mathcal{C}_{free}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude hardness.**
Since TQBF is PSPACE-hard, and we have a polynomial-time reduction to GMPP:

$$
\mathrm{TQBF} \leq_p \mathrm{GMPP}
$$

Therefore, GMPP is PSPACE-hard. Combining with the upper bound from Schwartz-Sharir cell decomposition (which uses only polynomial space), GMPP is PSPACE-complete for polyhedral obstacles in $\mathbb{R}^3$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to show hardness, the examiner expects you to *name* the source problem (TQBF), *describe* the reduction intuitively, and *state* the conclusion. Full formal reduction is not required unless specifically asked.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decidability Framework Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Robot R, Obstacles O, q_init, q_goal] --> B{Is R semi-algebraic?}
    B -- No --> C[Problem may be UNDECIDABLE]
    B -- Yes --> D[Is O a finite set?]
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Construct C-space and C_free]
    E --> F{Is C_free a semi-algebraic set?}
    F -- No --> C
    F -- Yes --> G{Apply Cell Decomposition}
    G --> H[Schwartz-Sharir Algorithm]
    H --> I[Build Roadmap]
    I --> J{Search Roadmap}
    J -- Path Found --> K[YES: Decidable and Plan Exists]
    J -- No Path --> L[NO: Path Does Not Exist]
    K --> M[Time: L^O(1) * s^d^O(n)]
    L --> M
    C --> N[Reif Undecidable Variants]
```

## 4.2 Complexity Hierarchy for Special Cases

```mermaid
graph TD
    R0[General GMPP] --> R1[Polyhedral Obstacles in R^3]
    R1 --> R2[Multiple Boxes in 2D]
    R1 --> R3[Single Robot Arm with n Joints]
    R2 --> R4[PSPACE-complete]
    R3 --> R5[PSPACE-hard]
    R0 --> R6[2D Point Robot in n Polygons]
    R6 --> R7[NP-hard]
    R0 --> R8[1D Point Robot]
    R8 --> R9[Polynomial Time]
    R0 --> R10[Non-algebraic Constraints]
    R10 --> R11[Undecidable]
```

## 4.3 Decidability Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start([Problem Instance]) --> Q1{Input Encoding?}
    Q1 -- Finite Algebraic --> A1[DECIDABLE]
    Q1 -- Infinite / Non-computable --> A2[UNDECIDABLE]
    A1 --> Q2{Output Requirement?}
    Q2 -- Existence Only --> B1[Polynomial Space]
    Q2 -- Optimal Path --> B2[NP-hard variants]
    Q2 -- Time-Optimal --> B3[PSPACE-complete]
    B1 --> End([Theoretical Algorithm Exists])
    B2 --> End
    B3 --> End
    A2 --> Stop([No Algorithm Possible])
```

## 4.4 Roadmap Construction Block Diagram

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 4
    block:input:1
        A["INPUT: Algebraic constraints p1 to ps"]
    end
    block:phase1:1
        B["PHASE 1: Critical Point Detection via Cylindrical Algebraic Decomposition"]
    end
    block:phase2:1
        C["PHASE 2: Silhouette Curve Sampling in C-space"]
    end
    block:phase3:1
        D["PHASE 3: Roadmap Graph Assembly and Connectivity Check"]
    end
    block:out:1
        E["OUTPUT: Roadmap R with q_init and q_goal in same component if path exists"]
    end
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram follows KTU Module 2 — it illustrates the canonical pipeline of any **exact** motion planning algorithm (Schwartz-Sharir / Canny).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define decidability in the context of the General Motion Planning Problem (GMPP). Why is GMPP decidable for semi-algebraic robots and obstacles?**

**Model Answer:**

Decidability of GMPP refers to the existence of a finite, terminating algorithm that can correctly determine, for any given instance $(\mathcal{R}, \mathcal{O}, \mathbf{q}_{init}, \mathbf{q}_{goal})$, whether a collision-free path exists. GMPP is decidable for semi-algebraic robots and obstacles because the configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ is a finite-dimensional semi-algebraic set, the free space $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is the complement of another semi-algebraic set (the configuration space obstacle $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$), and Tarski-Seidenberg's theorem guarantees that quantifier elimination over real-closed fields terminates. Hence, by the Schwartz-Sharir cell decomposition algorithm, an exact roadmap can be constructed in finite time, proving decidability.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1.5) + Justification with theorem name (1.5) = **3 Marks**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between decidability and tractability in motion planning with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

A problem is *decidable* if a correct answer can be computed in finite (but possibly super-polynomial) time. A problem is *tractable* if it can be solved in polynomial time. GMPP is decidable but PSPACE-hard, hence generally intractable. Example: For a 6-DOF robot among polyhedral obstacles, the Schwartz-Sharir algorithm decides existence in finite time (decidable), but the time complexity $L^{O(1)}(sd)^{O(n)}$ may be doubly-exponential in $d$, making it impractical (intractable). In contrast, the 1D point-robot motion planning is both decidable and tractable (polynomial time).

**Mark Distribution:** Decidability definition (1) + Tractability definition (1) + Example (1) = **3 Marks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** State and explain Reif's theorem on the complexity of the General Motion Planning Problem. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Describe the Schwartz-Sharir cell decomposition approach for deciding motion planning in semi-algebraic configuration spaces. Show that the algorithm terminates in finite time. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Reif's Theorem:**

**Statement (3 marks):**
Reif's theorem (1979) establishes that the General Motion Planning Problem (GMPP) for a movable object among a finite collection of polyhedral obstacles in $\mathbb{R}^3$ is **PSPACE-hard**. When combined with the polynomial-space upper bound from cell decomposition methods, the problem is **PSPACE-complete**.

**Explanation (4 marks):**
The proof of PSPACE-hardness proceeds via a polynomial-time reduction from the True Quantified Boolean Formula (TQBF) problem, which is the canonical PSPACE-complete problem. The reduction works as follows:

- Each universally quantified variable $\forall x_i$ in the TQBF instance is encoded as a corridor where the robot must successfully navigate both $x_i = 0$ and $x_i = 1$ branches.
- Each existentially quantified variable $\exists x_i$ is encoded as a branching corridor where only one branch must be traversed.
- The Boolean formula $\varphi$ is encoded as a set of obstacles arranged such that a collision corresponds to violating $\varphi$.

A collision-free path from $\mathbf{q}_{init}$ to $\mathbf{q}_{goal}$ exists if and only if the TQBF instance evaluates to TRUE. Since TQBF is PSPACE-hard, GMPP inherits this hardness.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse **PSPACE-hard** with **NP-hard**. PSPACE-hardness implies NP-hardness (since NP $\subseteq$ PSPACE) but is a strictly stronger statement. Writing "GMPP is NP-hard" loses 2 marks if the question specifically asks about Reif's PSPACE result.

---

**(b) Schwartz-Sharir Cell Decomposition:**

**Method (4 marks):**
The algorithm proceeds in three stages:

1. **Cylindrical Algebraic Decomposition (CAD):** Recursively project the polynomial constraints $p_1(\mathbf{q}), \dots, p_s(\mathbf{q})$ onto lower-dimensional subspaces until a 1D set is obtained. The projection lifts define a partition of $\mathcal{C}$-space into a finite number of cells, each of which is sign-invariant (every $p_i$ has constant sign on each cell).
2. **Roadmap Construction:** Within each cell, a single sample point is chosen. Cells are connected by arcs that cross cell boundaries transversally, forming a roadmap graph $G$.
3. **Search:** Run a graph search (e.g., BFS/DFS) from $\mathbf{q}_{init}$ to determine the connected component containing the goal.

**Termination Proof (3 marks):**
By Tarski-Seidenberg's theorem, quantifier elimination over the reals is effective and produces a finite quantifier-free formula. Therefore, the recursive projection terminates after a finite number of steps. The number of cells produced is bounded by $(sd)^{O(n)}$ (Warren's theorem), which is finite. Hence the algorithm terminates in finite time with a correct yes/no answer, establishing **decidability**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Define Configuration Space ($\mathcal{C}$-space) and Configuration Free Space ($\mathcal{C}_{free}$). Discuss how these concepts relate to decidability. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the pipeline of an exact motion planning algorithm. Why are exact algorithms rarely used in practice despite being decidable? *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Definitions (3 marks):**

The **Configuration Space** $\mathcal{C}$ of a robot is the manifold of all possible configurations $\mathbf{q}$ the robot can attain. For a rigid body in $\mathbb{R}^2$, $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ (translation + rotation); for an $n$-joint revolute arm, $\mathcal{C} = T^n$.

The **Configuration Free Space** is:

$$
\mathcal{C}_{free} = \{ \mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{R}(\mathbf{q}) \cap \mathcal{O} = \emptyset \}
$$

And the **Configuration Obstacle** is its complement:

$$
\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \{ \mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{R}(\mathbf{q}) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset \}
$$

**Relation to Decidability (4 marks):**
Decidability of GMPP reduces to the question: *Is the connected component of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ containing $\mathbf{q}_{init}$ also containing $\mathbf{q}_{goal}$?* When $\mathcal{R}$ and $\mathcal{O}$ are semi-algebraic, $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is itself a semi-algebraic set. By the Tarski-Seidenberg transfer principle, this set has a finite description. Hence:

- $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ admits a finite cell decomposition (Schwartz-Sharir).
- Connectivity of a semi-algebraic set is decidable via roadmap construction (Canny).
- Therefore, the existence question is decidable.

Without semi-algebraicity (e.g., involving transcendental functions or non-computable sets), $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ may not be constructible, and the problem may become **undecidable**.

---

**(b) Pipeline of an Exact Algorithm (4 marks):**

Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.4**. The pipeline consists of:

1. **Input Encoding:** Robot $\mathcal{R}$ and obstacles $\mathcal{O}$ as polynomial inequalities.
2. **Cell Decomposition:** Recursive projection via CAD to decompose $\mathcal{C}$-space.
3. **Roadmap Construction:** Connect cell samples via transversal arcs.
4. **Search:** BFS/DFS to check connectivity from $\mathbf{q}_{init}$ to $\mathbf{q}_{goal}$.
5. **Output:** YES (with path) or NO (path does not exist).

**Why Not Used in Practice (3 marks):**
Despite being decidable, exact algorithms have worst-case time complexity $(sd)^{O(n)}$, which is **doubly-exponential** in the C-space dimension $d$. For modern robotic systems (6+ DOF arms, mobile manipulators), $d \geq 6$ and $n \geq 10$, making exact algorithms computationally infeasible. Consequently, practitioners use **sampling-based planners** like PRM (Probabilistic Roadmap) and RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree), which trade completeness guarantees for probabilistic completeness and practical runtime.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** When answering "why not exact algorithms?", students often just write "they are slow." Examiners expect mention of **specific complexity bounds** (e.g., doubly-exponential in $d$) and the **alternative** used in practice (sampling-based planners). A vague answer loses up to 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones for Decidability Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing decidability with tractability** — both are distinct concepts.
> 2. **Writing "NP-hard" instead of "PSPACE-hard"** for general GMPP — loses accuracy marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the role of Tarski-Seidenberg theorem** — this is the *root* of all decidability proofs.
> 4. **Not mentioning the bit-length $L$** in complexity bounds — KTU expects $L^{O(1)}(sd)^{O(n)}$, not just $(sd)^{O(n)}$.
> 5. **Skipping the schematic** in 14-mark answers — KTU awards 1–2 marks for neat diagrams.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GMPP** stands for General Motion Planning Problem (Piano Mover's Problem).
- GMPP is **decidable** (Reif 1979) for semi-algebraic robots and obstacles in $\mathbb{R}^d$.
- GMPP is **PSPACE-hard** for polyhedral obstacles in $\mathbb{R}^3$, and **PSPACE-complete** for polyhedral robots in $\mathbb{R}^3$.
- **2D point robot** among $n$ polygons is **NP-hard**.
- **1D motion** planning is in **P** (polynomial time).
- The **Schwartz-Sharir algorithm** decides GMPP in time $L^{O(1)}(sd)^{O(n)}$ — exponential in degree $n$, polynomial in bit-length $L$.
- **Canny's roadmap** has size $(sd)^{O(n)}$ — doubly-exponential in $d$.
- **Decidability rests on Tarski-Seidenberg's transfer principle** for real-closed fields.
- **Non-algebraic obstacles** can make the problem **undecidable**.
- **Exact algorithms** are PSPACE-complete; **sampling-based algorithms** (RRT, PRM) are practical but only probabilistically complete.
- **Configuration space dimension** for a free-flying 2D rigid body is $\dim(\mathcal{C}) = 3$ (x, y, $\theta$).
- **Configuration space dimension** for an $n$-revolute-joint planar arm is $\dim(\mathcal{C}) = n$.
- $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is a semi-algebraic set when both robot and obstacles are semi-algebraic.
- $\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \{ \mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{R}(\mathbf{q}) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset \}$.
- Always mention both $\mathcal{R}$ and $\mathcal{O}$ as semi-algebraic when stating decidability theorems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Mnemonic for Decidability:**
> *"Decidable = Yes, exists in finite time. Tractable = Polynomial time. GMPP = Decidable but PSPACE-hard. Schwartz-Sharir proves it. Canny optimises it. Reif started it all."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Connectivity
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Connectivity

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Robot Motion Planning, **Connectivity** is a fundamental topological property of the **Free Configuration Space** ($\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$) that determines whether a continuous motion (path) exists between any two configurations of the robot without colliding with obstacles.

Formally, a topological space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is said to be **connected** if it cannot be expressed as the union of two or more disjoint non-empty open subsets. More strongly, $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is **path-connected** if, for any two configurations $q_1, q_2 \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$, there exists a continuous function (path)

$$\tau : [0,1] \longrightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$$

such that $\tau(0) = q_1$ and $\tau(1) = q_2$, where $\tau(t) \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ for all $t \in [0,1]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Connectivity is the bridge between the *geometric* description of the robot and the *topological* structure of its free space. If $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is not connected, the motion planning problem has **no feasible solution** for configurations lying in different connected components.

## 1.2 Components of Connectivity in Motion Planning

Connectivity manifests at three distinct levels in the motion planning pipeline:

1. **Topological Connectivity of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$** — The macroscopic property defining whether the free space forms a single continuous region or multiple disjoint "islands."
2. **Graph Connectivity of the Roadmap** — Once a discrete graph (Visibility, Voronoi, PRM, etc.) is built, every edge and vertex must preserve connectivity of the underlying continuous space.
3. **Path Connectivity (Homotopy)** — Two paths are considered equivalent if one can be continuously deformed into the other without crossing obstacles.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — The Flooded City:**
> Imagine a city (the configuration space) partially flooded by lakes representing obstacles. $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is the **dry land**. Connectivity asks: *"Can I walk from my house to the school entirely on dry land without swimming?"* If the school sits on a separate dry patch surrounded by water, the city is **disconnected** — no walking path exists. The motion planner's job is to first *detect* which land patches (connected components) belong together, and then *find* a continuous route within a single connected component.

## 1.4 Key Quantitative Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Connected Components | $N_c$ | Count of disjoint path-connected regions in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ |
| Roadmap Edges | $\vert E \vert$ | Number of edges in the connectivity graph |
| Roadmap Vertices | $\vert V \vert$ | Number of nodes (milestones) in the roadmap |
| Graph Connectivity (algebraic) | $\kappa(G)$ | Minimum vertex/edge cut required to disconnect $G$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard assumption in Module 2: $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is treated as an **open subset** of $\mathcal{C}$, ensuring the configuration space topology inherits from $\mathbb{R}^n$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Hierarchy of Connectivity

Connectivity in topology is layered from weakest to strongest. Understanding this hierarchy is essential for answering KTU questions on the *strength* of the connectivity assumption used.

1. **Connected** — Cannot be partitioned into two separated non-empty open sets.
2. **Path-Connected** — Every pair of points admits a continuous path.  
   $$\forall\, q_1, q_2 \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}},\; \exists\, \tau : [0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$$
3. **Simply Connected** — Path-connected **and** every loop can be continuously contracted to a point (no "holes" of genus $g \geq 1$).
4. **Multiply Connected** — Path-connected but contains $g \geq 1$ topological holes.

**Implication for Motion Planning:**

$$\text{Path-Connected} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Connected}$$

but the converse **does not** hold in general topological spaces (it does hold for manifolds, which $\mathcal{C}$ typically is).

## 2.2 Connected Components of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$

The free configuration space can be uniquely partitioned into a set of maximal connected subsets called **path-connected components**:

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \bigsqcup_{i=1}^{N_c} \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}^{(i)}$$

where $\bigsqcup$ denotes disjoint union and each $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}^{(i)}$ is itself path-connected.

- Two configurations $q_1 \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}^{(i)}$ and $q_2 \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}^{(j)}$ with $i \neq j$ are **not connected** by any collision-free path.
- The **Quotient Space** $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} / {\sim}$ (where $q_1 \sim q_2$ iff they lie in the same component) becomes a discrete space of $N_c$ points — the **Connectivity Graph**.

## 2.3 Roadmap Connectivity Preservation

A roadmap $\mathcal{R} \subseteq \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is a 1-dimensional graph $(V, E)$ embedded in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. For the roadmap to be a **valid representation** of connectivity, three preservation conditions must hold:

- **Accessibility:** From any $q \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$, a simple path exists to some vertex $v \in V$.
- **Departability:** From any $v \in V$, a simple path exists to any $q \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.
- **Connectivity:** The graph $(V, E)$ has the same number of connected components as $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.

$$\kappa(\mathcal{R}) \;\longleftrightarrow\; \kappa(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}})$$

where $\kappa(\cdot)$ denotes the *component-counting* invariant.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Path-Connectedness | $\exists\, \tau \in C^0,\; \tau(0)=q_1,\; \tau(1)=q_2$ | Continuous path |
| Connected Components | $N_c = \vert \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} / {\sim} \vert$ | Discrete count |
| Graph Connectivity | $\kappa(G) = \min \vert S \vert$ s.t. $G \setminus S$ is disconnected | Vertices or edges |
| Euler Characteristic | $\chi = V - E + F = 2 - 2g - h$ | Topological invariant |
| Genus (holes) | $g = 1 - \frac{\chi}{2}$ for closed orientable surface | Integer $\geq 0$ |
| Roadmap Edge Count (Visibility) | $\vert E \vert \leq \binom{n_v}{2}$ | $n_v$ = vertices of obstacles |
| PRM Coverage | $P(\text{connected}) \to 1$ as $\vert V \vert \to \infty$ | Asymptotic |

> **Critical LaTeX Note:** All set cardinalities use $\vert \cdot \vert$, written **without** the keyboard pipe character to prevent table-rendering errors.

## 2.5 Why Connectivity Matters in Engineering

- **Industrial Manipulators:** When a robot arm is mounted near a wall, certain joint configurations may become inaccessible — splitting $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ into disjoint components. Connectivity analysis detects this *before* path planning begins.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Disconnected free-space components indicate regions the vehicle simply cannot reach (e.g., separated parking lots in a road network).
- **Surgical Robots:** Connectivity must be preserved during *real-time* re-planning when the anatomy deforms — a failure here is life-critical.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Connected Components from Euler's Formula

For a 2D polygonal free space decomposed into a planar roadmap graph, we can compute the number of "faces" (regions) and deduce the topological genus.

**Given:**
- $V$ roadmap vertices
- $E$ roadmap edges
- $F$ roadmap faces (including the unbounded outer face)

**Planar Euler Relation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V - E + F &= 2 \quad \text{(connected planar graph, genus } g=0\text{)} \\
V - E + F &= 2 - 2g \quad \text{(general orientable surface)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step-by-Step Transition:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Start with Euler's formula for a planar graph:} \\
& V - E + F = 2 \\
\\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Introduce a correction term } 2g \text{ for surfaces of genus } g: \\
& V - E + F = 2 - 2g \\
\\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Solve for the genus (number of topological holes):} \\
& 2g = 2 - V + E - F \\
\\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Final explicit formula for genus:} \\
& g = \frac{2 - V + E - F}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

> *A polygonal roadmap in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ has $V = 12$ vertices, $E = 18$ edges, and $F = 5$ faces. Determine the genus.*

$$\begin{aligned}
g &= \frac{2 - V + E - F}{2} \\
&= \frac{2 - 12 + 18 - 5}{2} \\
&= \frac{3}{2} \\
&= 1.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{INVALID (not integer)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> If $g$ is **not an integer**, the input $(V, E, F)$ is inconsistent — meaning the graph as described **cannot** be embedded in an orientable surface. In KTU board exams, this signals an arithmetic or topological-claim error.

> *Correction:* Assuming $V = 10$, $E = 18$, $F = 8$:

$$g = \frac{2 - 10 + 18 - 8}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1$$

A genus of $g = 1$ indicates a **torus-like hole** in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ — a multiply-connected space.

## 3.2 Derivation: Probability of PRM Connectivity

For a **Probabilistic Roadmap (PRM)**, Milestone connectivity is governed by visibility sampling.

**Step 1 — Single-edge connection probability** (between two random samples $q_i, q_j$):

$$p = \text{Prob}\bigl[\text{straight-line } \overline{q_i q_j} \subseteq \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}\bigr]$$

**Step 2 — Expected number of edges per node:**

$$\mathbb{E}[\deg(v)] = (N - 1) \cdot p$$

where $N = \vert V \vert$ is the total number of milestones.

**Step 3 — For the graph to remain connected as $N \to \infty$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{graph connected}) &= 1 - P(\text{isolated node}) \\
&\geq 1 - N \cdot (1 - p)^{N - 1} \\
&\xrightarrow{N \to \infty} 1 \quad \text{if } p \text{ is bounded below by } p_0 > 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic of each conversion row:**

- **Step 1:** A connection exists iff the **straight-line local planner** sees no obstacle — standard assumption in PRM theory.
- **Step 2:** Each of the $(N-1)$ other nodes may link to $v$ with probability $p$; expectation is linear.
- **Step 3:** The probability that a node remains isolated falls exponentially; multiplied by $N$ it still vanishes, giving *asymptotic almost-sure connectivity*.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Connectivity Tests

| Test | Input | Output | Sufficient? | Necessary? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Set Partition Test | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = U_1 \cup U_2$, $U_1 \cap U_2 = \emptyset$ | Disconnected iff both $U_1, U_2$ open & non-empty | ✓ | ✓ |
| Path Existence | $q_1, q_2$ | Connected iff $\exists$ continuous $\tau$ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Graph Component Count | $(V, E)$ | Disconnected iff $\kappa(G) = 0$ | For roadmaps | ✓ |
| Homology Computation | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ | $H_0(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}) = \mathbb{Z}^{N_c}$ | Algebraic | ✓ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Connectivity Hierarchy Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Topological Space C_free] --> B{Connected?}
    B -- No --> B1[Disconnected: Multiple Components]
    B -- Yes --> C{Path-Connected?}
    C -- No --> C1[Connected but not Path-Connected]
    C -- Yes --> D{Simply Connected?}
    D -- No --> D1[Multiply Connected - Has Holes]
    D -- Yes --> D2[Contractible - No Holes]
    B1 --> E[Component Count N_c]
    C1 --> E
    D1 --> F[Genus g greater than or equal to 1]
    D2 --> G[Genus equals 0]
    E --> H[Quotient Space Connectivity Graph]
    F --> I[Euler Characteristic Computation]
    G --> I
```

## 4.2 Roadmap Connectivity Preservation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER]
        A1[Robot Geometry]
        A2[Obstacle Description]
        A3[Start q_s and Goal q_g]
    end

    subgraph PROC[PROCESSING LAYER]
        B1[Sampler - Random or Deterministic]
        B2[Collision Checker]
        B3[Local Planner - Straight Line]
        B4[Connectivity Validator]
    end

    subgraph OUT[OUTPUT LAYER]
        C1[Roadmap Graph V E]
        C2[Component Labeling]
        C3[Path Query Engine]
    end

    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B2
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    A3 --> C3
```

## 4.3 Disconnected $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ — Schematic Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SP[Connected Component 1]
        S1((Start q_s)) --- N1((V1))
        N1 --- N2((V2))
        N2 --- N3((V3))
        N1 --- N3
    end

    subgraph OB[Obstacle Region - Separating]
        O1[Forbidden Zone]
    end

    subgraph GP[Connected Component 2]
        M1((V4)) --- M2((V5))
        M2 --- G1((Goal q_g))
        M1 --- G1
        M3((V6)) --- M2
    end

    SP -. "No Edge Exists" .- OB
    OB -. "No Edge Exists" .- GP
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The dashed crossing lines over the obstacle explicitly indicate the absence of any roadmap edge — visually encoding that $q_s$ and $q_g$ lie in **different connected components**, so no collision-free path exists.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**  
> Define *path-connectedness* of a configuration space. How does it differ from mere connectedness in the context of motion planning?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is **path-connected** if for every pair of configurations $q_1, q_2 \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$, there exists a continuous function $\tau : [0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ with $\tau(0) = q_1$ and $\tau(1) = q_2$.

**Key difference from connectedness (1 mark each):**
- Path-connectedness is a **strictly stronger** property than connectedness. Every path-connected space is connected, but not vice-versa (counter-example: the *topologist's sine curve*).
- In motion planning, path-connectedness is the **physically relevant** property: it guarantees the existence of a feasible trajectory, not just a topological set-theoretic link. (1 mark for the motion-planning relevance.)

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**  
> What is a *connected component* of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$? Mention its significance in roadmap-based planning.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **connected component** $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}^{(i)}$ of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is a maximal path-connected subset — i.e., it cannot be enlarged while preserving path-connectedness. The free space decomposes as:

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \bigsqcup_{i=1}^{N_c} \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}^{(i)} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Significance in roadmap planning (2 marks):**
- The number $N_c$ of components determines **reachability**: a goal in a different component is unreachable.
- The quotient graph $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} / {\sim}$ (one node per component) becomes a discrete connectivity map.
- Pre-computing $N_c$ allows a planner to reject infeasible queries instantly, saving computation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Model – Module 2 Internal Choice Set]**  
> **(a)** Explain in detail the three conditions that a roadmap must satisfy to preserve the connectivity of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. Illustrate with a suitable block diagram. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** For a planar roadmap constructed over $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ of a 2-DOF mobile robot, the graph has $V = 15$ vertices, $E = 24$ edges, and $F = 11$ faces. Determine the genus of the underlying surface and comment on the connectivity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

The three preservation conditions are:

1. **Accessibility** (2 marks): From any configuration $q \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$, there exists a simple path $\tau$ lying entirely in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ that connects $q$ to at least one vertex $v \in V$ of the roadmap.
2. **Departability** (2 marks): From any vertex $v \in V$, there exists a simple path $\tau \subseteq \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ that reaches any configuration $q \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.
3. **Connectivity Equivalence** (3 marks): The number of connected components of the roadmap graph $(V, E)$ must equal $N_c$, the number of connected components of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. Formally:

$$\text{CC}(V, E) = \text{CC}(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}})$$

where $\text{CC}(\cdot)$ denotes the connected-component count.

**Block diagram** — refer to Section 4.2 of these notes (Roadmap Connectivity Preservation Block Diagram). (Validation/processing block demonstrates accessibility, departability, and connectivity check.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
g &= \frac{2 - V + E - F}{2} \\
&= \frac{2 - 15 + 24 - 11}{2} \\
&= \frac{0}{2} \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step-by-step:**

- Step 1 (1 mark): Write Euler's formula $V - E + F = 2 - 2g$ and rearrange for $g$.
- Step 2 (2 marks): Substitute $V = 15$, $E = 24$, $F = 11$.
- Step 3 (1 mark): Compute numerator: $2 - 15 + 24 - 11 = 0$.
- Step 4 (1 mark): Obtain $g = 0$.
- Step 5 (2 marks): **Comment** — Genus $g = 0$ implies $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is **simply connected** (topologically equivalent to a disc) — there are no "holes" and the free space is a single connected component, $N_c = 1$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Internal Choice)

> **(a)** With the aid of a neat diagram, explain the concept of *connected components* in the free configuration space. Why is component-counting considered the first preprocessing step in motion planning? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Probabilistic Roadmap (PRM) is constructed in a workspace where the straight-line local planner has a success probability $p = 0.05$ per pair. If $N = 200$ milestones are sampled, estimate the expected degree of each node and the probability that a randomly chosen node is isolated. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Concept (3 marks):** A connected component of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is a maximal subset $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}^{(i)}$ such that any two points within it can be joined by a continuous collision-free path. The free space partitions uniquely:

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \bigsqcup_{i=1}^{N_c} \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}^{(i)}$$

**Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.3 (Disconnected $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ Schematic Topology) — showing two disjoint components separated by an obstacle.

**Why preprocessing (2 marks):** Counting $N_c$ *before* path search allows the planner to:
- Reject queries with $q_s$ and $q_g$ in different components (infeasible).
- Allocate roadmap construction only within the relevant component.
- Provide a discrete *connectivity signature* of the workspace for higher-level planning.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Expected degree (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[\deg(v)] &= (N - 1) \cdot p \\
&= (200 - 1) \cdot 0.05 \\
&= 199 \cdot 0.05 \\
&= 9.95
\end{aligned}$$

So on average, each milestone connects to approximately **10 neighbours**.

**Step 2 — Probability of isolation (4 marks):**

A node is isolated iff **none** of the $N - 1 = 199$ potential edges succeed:

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{isolated}) &= (1 - p)^{N - 1} \\
&= (1 - 0.05)^{199} \\
&= (0.95)^{199}
\end{aligned}$$

Using logarithms:

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln(0.95^{199}) &= 199 \cdot \ln(0.95) \\
&= 199 \cdot (-0.05129) \\
&= -10.206
\end{aligned}$$

$$P(\text{isolated}) = e^{-10.206} \approx 3.67 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** Approximately **0.0037%** of nodes are isolated — the graph is well-connected for $N = 200$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Connectivity Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "connected" with "path-connected"** (loses 1–2 marks). Always quote the *path* definition when discussing motion planning.
> 2. **Sign errors in Euler's formula rearrangement.** The correct rearrangement is $g = \frac{2 - V + E - F}{2}$. Many students write $g = \frac{V - E + F - 2}{2}$, which gives the *negative* of the correct genus.
> 3. **Ignoring the outer face.** When counting $F$, remember the unbounded exterior region *is* a face. Omitting it produces an off-by-one error and a non-integer genus.
> 4. **Forgetting to verify reachability across components.** Even when $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is disconnected, students often attempt to "find" a path — a logical contradiction worth losing 3 marks.
> 5. **Treating $(1-p)^{N-1}$ as $(1-p^N)$.** The independence assumption applies to each of the $(N-1)$ edges, not to $N$ edges.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Connectivity** of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ determines whether a motion planning problem is **feasible at all**.
- A space is **path-connected** if any two points admit a continuous path — this is the *motion-planning relevant* notion.
- **Connected components** form the quotient space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} / {\sim}$; their count $N_c$ is the **first preprocessing step**.
- **Roadmap validity** requires Accessibility, Departability, and Connectivity Equivalence with $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.
- **Euler's formula** $V - E + F = 2 - 2g$ lets us compute genus and infer simple vs. multiply connected free spaces.
- **PRM connectivity** becomes almost-sure as $N \to \infty$, provided $p > 0$ is bounded away from zero.
- Always remember: **path-connected ⇒ connected**, but not conversely (except on manifolds).
- Standard metric $\kappa(G)$ = minimum vertex/edge cut — used in roadmap *robustness* analysis.
- A *disconnected* $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ means **no path exists** — the planning problem should be rejected, not retried.
- In KTU board exams, **draw a diagram** for any connectivity question worth ≥ 7 marks — it typically earns 1–2 free marks.
